steps.description: Set out what must be proved
steps.explanation: The right-hand side is a single reciprocal function, so the plan is to write every function on the left in terms of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$, simplify, and watch the common factors cancel.
Converting to sine and cosine is the most reliable way to simplify a mixed expression, because everything is then built from just two quantities.
Writing $1$ as $\dfrac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta}$ lets the two terms combine over the common denominator $\cos\theta$.
Replacing $\tan\theta$ by $\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$ prepares the denominator for the same common-denominator treatment.

steps.description: Combine the denominator over a common denominator
steps.explanation: Both terms are placed over $\cos\theta$, ready to be factorised.
Taking out the common factor $\sin\theta$ reveals a $(\cos\theta+1)$ bracket that matches the numerator, which is the key to the cancellation.
Substituting the simplified numerator and denominator gives a fraction divided by a fraction.

steps.description: Divide by multiplying by the reciprocal
steps.explanation: Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal, which lines up the matching factors for cancelling.
The $\cos\theta$ in the denominator of the first fraction cancels the $\cos\theta$ in the numerator of the second.
The factor $(\cos\theta+1)$ appears top and bottom, so it cancels, leaving just $\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}$.
By definition $\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}$ is $\operatorname{cosec}\theta$, which is exactly the right-hand side.
The cancellations require these quantities to be non-zero, so the identity holds everywhere both sides are defined.
At $60^\circ$ the left-hand side evaluates to $\dfrac{2}{\sqrt3}$, and $\operatorname{cosec} 60^\circ=\dfrac{1}{\sin 60^\circ}=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt3}$ agrees, confirming the algebra.
The left-hand side simplifies exactly to $\operatorname{cosec}\theta$ using only reciprocal definitions and factorising, so the identity is proved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Writing everything over $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ gives $\dfrac{(\cos\theta+1)/\cos\theta}{\sin\theta(\cos\theta+1)/\cos\theta}=\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}=\operatorname{cosec}\theta$, as required.
